back: Cache efficiency measures how effectively the cache memory reduces memory access time. It is typically expressed as the cache hit rate (percentage of memory accesses served by the cache) or its complement, the miss rate.

front: Define cache hit rate and cache miss rate
back: Cache hit rate is the fraction of memory accesses found in the cache (hits/total accesses). Cache miss rate is 1 - hit rate, representing accesses not found in cache that must fetch from main memory.

Cache miss penalty is the additional time or cycles required to fetch data from main memory when a cache miss occurs. It includes the time to access the lower memory level and transfer the data to the cache.

front: List the three types of cache misses
back:
1) Compulsory (cold) miss - first access to a block
2) Capacity miss - cache cannot hold all needed blocks
3) Conflict miss - multiple blocks map to same cache line (in set-associative or direct-mapped caches)
Temporal locality is the principle that recently accessed memory locations are likely to be accessed again soon. Cache exploits this by keeping recently accessed data closer to the CPU.

Spatial locality is the tendency to access memory locations near recently accessed locations. Cache exploits this by fetching blocks (cache lines) containing multiple bytes, not just the requested byte.

front: Compare direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully-associative caches
back:
Direct-mapped: each memory block maps to one cache line (simple, fast, more conflicts)
Set-associative: blocks map to a set of n lines (balanced)
Fully-associative: any block can go anywhere (most flexible, expensive hardware)

front: What is the formula for Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)?
back:
AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty)
Where Hit time is time to access data on cache hit, Miss rate is probability of miss, and Miss penalty is extra time on miss.

Write-through: writes go to both cache and main memory simultaneously (simple, consistent, but slow)
Write-back: writes only update cache, main memory updated when block replaced (fast, but complex)

1) LRU (Least Recently Used) - evicts least recently accessed block
2) FIFO (First In First Out) - evicts oldest block
3) Random - evicts random block
4) LFU (Least Frequently Used) - evicts least used block
A cache line is the minimum unit of data transferred between cache and main memory. Typical sizes are 16-64 bytes. It contains the requested data and its address tag.

Cache coherency ensures that multiple caches (in multi-core systems) have consistent views of memory. When one core modifies data, other cores must see the updated value or invalidate their cached copies.
Victim cache is a small fully-associative cache that stores blocks evicted from the main cache. It helps reduce miss penalty by providing a quick backup location for recently displaced blocks.
Hardware prefetching is a technique where the hardware predicts future memory accesses and loads data into cache before it is requested, reducing miss penalty through speculative fetching.

front: How is cache efficiency measured in microcontrollers?
back: In microcontrollers, cache efficiency is measured using: hit/miss ratios, execution time benchmarks, power consumption analysis, and real-time response metrics. Tools like hardware performance counters and simulation tools are used.
